epigraph.text:
The Lord is my light and my salvation—whom shall 
I fear?
epigraph.quote: Measure what is measurable, and make measurable what is not so.

content.text: {Measure what is measurable, and make measurable what is not so.}{Galileo Galilei}

Some quadratic equations have a simpler form: no middle term. For these, 
we can solve by taking square roots—a direct and efficient approach.

content.title: Square Root Principle
content.content:
If $x^2 = k$ where $k \geq 0$, then:
\[
 x = \sqrt{k} \quad \text{or} \quad x = -\sqrt{k}
\]
Written concisely: $x = \pm\sqrt{k}$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Don't forget the $\pm$! The equation $x^2 = 9$ has two solutions: 
$x = 3$ and $x = -3$, because both $3^2 = 9$ and $(-3)^2 = 9$.

content.text: First isolate the $x^2$ term before taking square roots.
When $k$ is not a perfect square, the solutions are irrational numbers.
The square root method works when any expression is squared.

content.title: Expression squared
content.solution:
Take square roots:
\[
 x - 3 = \pm 4
\]

Solve each:
$$
 x - 3 = 4 &\implies x = 7 \\
 x - 3 = -4 &\implies x = -1
$$

The solutions are $x = 7$ and $x = -1$.

content.title: More examples
content.solution:
1. $x + 5 = \pm 7 \implies x = 2$ or $x = -12$

2. $2x - 1 = \pm 3 \implies 2x = 4$ or $2x = -2 \implies x = 2$ or $x = -1$

3. $x - 4 = \pm\sqrt{12} = \pm 2\sqrt{3} \implies x = 4 \pm 2\sqrt{3}$

content.title: No real solution
content.solution:
There is no real number whose square is negative. The equation has 
**no real solution**.

(In more advanced courses, you'll learn about imaginary numbers that 
provide solutions like $x = \pm 3i$.)

marginNotes.text:
I will give you hidden treasures, riches 
stored in secret places.

marginNotes.author: Pythagoras
marginNotes.text:
Number is the ruler of forms and ideas, and 
the cause of gods and daemons.
